questions.question: Define a 'rational agent'. Why doesn't rationality require omniscience?
expectedPoints: Definition: An agent that chooses actions that maximize its expected performance measure given its percept sequence and prior knowledge.
Rationality depends on available information, not actual outcomes.
An agent can be rational without knowing the future or the entire state of the world (i.e., without being omniscient).
It makes the best decision based on what it knows and perceives.

questions.question: Compare and contrast a Simple Reflex Agent with a Model-Based Reflex Agent. Illustrate your answer with a suitable example for each.
expectedPoints: Simple Reflex Agent: Acts based on current percept only. Uses condition-action rules (if-then).
Limitation: Fails in partially observable environments. Example: A simple thermostat that turns on AC if temperature > 25°C.
Model-Based Reflex Agent: Maintains an internal state to track aspects of the world not visible in the current percept.
This internal state is updated using a model of how the world works.
Advantage: Can handle partial observability. Example: A vacuum cleaner agent that remembers which rooms it has already cleaned.
Conclusion: Model-based agents are more powerful and applicable to a wider range of problems.
